intentional_test_features: Prolonged unremitting fever >5 days plus mucocutaneous signs (conjunctivitis, cracked lips, strawberry tongue), polymorphous rash, extremity changes and cervical lymphadenopathy - the AI Doctor must recognise complete Kawasaki disease, a rare but critical paediatric diagnosis
Antipyretic-refractory fever is a key clue: parents alternating paracetamol/ibuprofen with no effect over 6 days should heighten, not lower, suspicion

Acute cardiac involvement red flags: sudden chest clutching, tachycardia out of proportion to fever, and a new murmur raise concern for myocarditis/coronary involvement - a paediatric emergency requiring immediate transfer
Time-critical treatment: IVIG must be started promptly (ideally within 10 days, urgently once cardiac involvement is suspected) to prevent coronary artery aneurysms and myocardial infarction

Mimic discrimination: scarlet fever, viral exanthem and measles must be distinguished, but the full mucocutaneous/extremity tetrad with disproportionate tachycardia and a new murmur points to Kawasaki with cardiac involvement

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-03)
